coredata.dc:title: Multi-mode application of graphene quantum dots bonded silica stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qi
Sun, Yaming
Zhang, Xiaoli
Zhang, Xia
Dong, Shuqing
Qiu, Hongdeng
Wang, Litao
Zhao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which possess hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding properties, have great prospect in HPLC. In this study, a novel GQDs bonded silica stationary phase was prepared and applied in multiple separation modes including normal phase, reversed phase and hydrophilic chromatography mode. Alkaloids, nucleosides and nucleobases were chosen as test compounds to evaluate the separation performance of this column in hydrophilic chromatographic mode. The tested polar compounds achieved baseline separation and the resolutions reached 2.32, 4.62, 7.79, 1.68 for thymidine, uridine, adenosine, cytidine and guanosine. This new column showed satisfactory chromatographic performance for anilines, phenols and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in normal and reversed phase mode. Five anilines were completely separated within 10min under the condition of mobile phase containing only 10% methanol. The effect of water content, buffer concentration and pH on chromatographic separation was further investigated, founding that this new stationary phase showed a complex retention mechanism of partitioning, adsorption and electrostatic interaction in hydrophilic chromatography mode, and the multiple retention interactions such as π-π stacking and π-π electron-donor-acceptor interaction played an important role during the separation process. This GQDs bonded column, which allows us to adjust appropriate chromatography mode according to the properties of analytes, has possibility in actual application after further research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stationary phases
Graphene quantum dots
Multiple retention interactions
Hydrophilic interaction chromatography

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of water content on retention
The effect of buffer concentration on retention
The effect of buffer pH on retention

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic process of GQDs bonded silica stationary phase.
TEM image of the prepared GQDs.
FTIR spectra of aminosilica and GQDs bonded silica.

(a-b) The confocal fluorescence microscope images of aminosilica under 460nm excitation and bright field, respectively. (c-d) The confocal fluorescence microscope images of GQDs bonded silica under 460nm excitation and bright field, respectively.

Chromatograms for the separation of (A) anilines and (B) phenols on (a) GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Conditions: (A) mobile phase, (a) 10% methanol/H2O (v/v); (b) 70% methanol/H2O (v/v); (B) mobile phase, (a) 10% methanol/H2O (v/v); (b) 50% methanol/H2O (v/v); flow rate, 0.8mL/min; injection volume, 10μL; UV detection, 254nm. Peak identity: (A) (1) p-methylaniline; (2) m-nitroaniline; (3) o-nitroaniline; (4) 1-naphthylamine; (5) diphenylamine; (B) (1) phenol; (2) 3,5-dimethylphenol; (3) p-tert-butylphenol; (4) p-nitrophenol; (5) 2-naphthol.

Chromatograms for the separation of (A) PAHs and (B) aromatic ethers on (a) GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Conditions: (A) mobile phase, (a) 40% methanol/H2O (v/v); (b) 70% methanol/H2O (v/v); flow rate, (a) 0.5mL/min; (b) 0.8mL/min; (B) mobile phase, (a) 1% methanol/H2O (v/v); (b) 60% methanol/H2O (v/v); flow rate, 0.8mL/min; injection volume, 10μL; UV detection, 254nm. Peak identity: (A) (1) naphthalene; (2) acenaphthene; (3) fluorene; (4) phenanthrene; (B) (1) benzene; (2) anisole; (3) o-nitroanisole.

Chromatograms for the separation of (A) anilines and (B) PAHs on (a) GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) silica column. Conditions: (A) mobile phase, (a) 20% isopropanol/n-hexane (v/v); (b) 5% isopropanol/n-hexane (v/v); (B) mobile phase, (a) 0.5% isopropanol/n-hexane (v/v); (b) 0.1% isopropanol/n-hexane; flow rate, 1.0mL/min; injection volume, 10μL; UV detection, 254nm. Peak identity: (A) (1) aniline; (2) 3,4-dimethylaniline; (3) m-nitroaniline; (4) p-nitroaniline; (B) (1) naphthalene; (2) fluorene; (3) anthracene; (4) 1,2-benzanthrene.

(a-b) Chromatograms for the separation of six alkaloids on GQDs/SiO2 column and NH2-SiO2 column: (1) caffeine, (2) theobromine, (3) isocorydine, (4) corydine, (5) protopine, (6) berberine; mobile phase: 90% acetonitrile/10% 10mM ammonium acetate, pH 6.8; flow rate: 1.0mL/min. (c) Chromatogram for the separation of five nucleosides on GQDs/SiO2 column: (1) thymidine, (2) uridine, (3) adenosine, (4) cytidine, (5) guanosine; mobile phase: 93% acetonitrile/7% 10mM ammonium acetate, pH 6.8; flow rate: 1.0mL/min. (d) Chromatogram for the separation of four nucleobases on GQDs/SiO2 column: (1) thymine, (2) adenine, (3) cytosine, (4) guanine; mobile phase: 90% acetonitrile/10% 10mM ammonium acetate, pH 6.8; flow rate: 1.0mL/min.

(A) Plot of log k versus the volume fraction of water in mobile phase. (B) Plot of log k versus logarithmic mole fraction of water in mobile phase. Mobile phase contained 10mM ammonium acetate, pH 6.8, UV detection: 254nm.
Selectivity factors (α) and resolutions (Rs) of anilines on different columns.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Selectivity factor, α=k2/k1. k: retention factor, k=(tR-t0)/t0, tR: retention time, t0: dead time.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Resolution, Rs=2(tR2-tR1)/(W1 +W2).W: width of peak base.

head.ce:title: Multi-mode application of graphene quantum dots bonded silica stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No.18, Tianshui Middle Road, Lanzhou, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which possess hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding properties, have great prospect in HPLC. In this study, a novel GQDs bonded silica stationary phase was prepared and applied in multiple separation modes including normal phase, reversed phase and hydrophilic chromatography mode. Alkaloids, nucleosides and nucleobases were chosen as test compounds to evaluate the separation performance of this column in hydrophilic chromatographic mode. The tested polar compounds achieved baseline separation and the resolutions reached 2.32, 4.62, 7.79, 1.68 for thymidine, uridine, adenosine, cytidine and guanosine. This new column showed satisfactory chromatographic performance for anilines, phenols and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in normal and reversed phase mode. Five anilines were completely separated within 10min under the condition of mobile phase containing only 10% methanol. The effect of water content, buffer concentration and pH on chromatographic separation was further investigated, founding that this new stationary phase showed a complex retention mechanism of partitioning, adsorption and electrostatic interaction in hydrophilic chromatography mode, and the multiple retention interactions such as π-π stacking and π-π electron-donor-acceptor interaction played an important role during the separation process. This GQDs bonded column, which allows us to adjust appropriate chromatography mode according to the properties of analytes, has possibility in actual application after further research.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [1–3], fullerene [4–6], nanodiamond [7–9] and graphene oxide (GO)/graphene (G) [10–12] have made great contributions to analytical chemistry [13]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, are composed of one or several graphene nanosheets with lateral dimensions smaller than 100nm [14]. They have attracted extensive attention in various disciplines because of excellent physics and chemistry characteristics such as good water solubility, unique fluorescent stability and good biocompatible. At present, they have been widely used for bioimaging, fluorescent sensing, organic photovoltaic device and separation science [15–18].

In separation science, GQDs have shown their superior adsorption performance. A Fe3O4/GQDs magnetic nanocomposite was prepared as efficient adsorbent for the detection of bisphenol A in water samples and it presented higher adsorption efficiency than Fe3O4/G composite [19]. A GQD-modified G adsorbent was produced and employed for ultrafast and effective adsorption of mixed toxic aromatic dyes from water. The RhB adsorption capacity of this composite adsorbent was significantly enhanced by a factor of three (497mg/g) compared with unmodified G [20]. The application of GQDs in chromatography stationary phases was rarely reported. Xiao and coworkers first introduced GQDs into the field of chromatography stationary phases [21]. They fabricated a GQDs coated capillary column used for gas chromatographic separation of alkanes and aromatic isomers. Compared with the commercial HP-5 and HP-innowax capillary columns, GQD coated columns allowed fast and efficient separation of the analytes at low temperature without temperature-programming. Zhao and coworkers investigated the separation performance of GQDs used as additives in capillary electrophoresis [17]. The resolutions of cinnamic acid and its derivatives increased with the addition of GQDs. Based on the predecessors’ work, we can see the potential of GQDs as stationary phase. However, to the best of our knowledge, the application of GQDs in HPLC stationary phases has not yet been reported. It is significant to investigate the separation performance of GQDs as HPLC stationary phase and compare the separation performance of GQDs based HPLC and other chromatographic techniques. GQDs have sufficient advantages to be used as HPLC stationary phases. First, GQDs have the same characteristics of other carbon nanomaterials, such as large specific surface area and good mechanical property, which make them possible to be used as chromatographic stationary phases. Second, GQDs possess the unique characteristics of high thermal stability, excellent solubility and small dimension, which make them easier to be immobilized on the silica support. Last, GQDs, not only have polyaromatic scaffold but also possess abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, have multifunctional properties such as hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding property. Regarding these characteristics, GQDs have great prospect in HPLC as multifunctional stationary phase.

In this work, to understand the chromatographic performance of GQDs as stationary phase component, GQDs were covalently bonded to the silica surface to form a novel HPLC stationary phase. Aromatic compounds including anilines, phenols and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) were chosen as probes to investigate its chromatographic performance and multiple retention mechanisms. C18 column and silica column were used as a comparison in reversed phase (RP) and normal phase (NP) mode, respectively. Alkaloids, nucleosides and nucleobases were selected as probes to investigate the hydrophilic performance of this new stationary phase in hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC) mode. Influencing factors, such as water content, buffer concentration and pH, were also studied to investigate the retention mechanism in HILIC mode.

Silica gel (300m2/g, 100Å, 5μm in diameter) was supplied by Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd (Japan). Graphite power, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and reagents (HPLC grade) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used for chromatographic separation were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China).

Elemental analysis was performed on vario EL (Elementar, Germany). The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra was acquired on an IFS120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). TEM images were obtained on a Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on a VG ESCALAB 210 instrument provided with a dual Mg/Mg anode X-ray source (Thermo, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) images were obtained on a FLUOVIEW FV1200 biological confocal laser scanning microscope (OLYMPUS, Japan). The data of specific surface area were obtained on the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms at 76K using an ASAP2010 surface analysis instrument (Micromeritics, USA).

GO aqueous dispersion was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [22,23]. GQDs were prepared from GO aqueous dispersion using a hydrothermal synthesis method with H2O2 as an etching agent and ammonia as an assistant [24]. First, 80mL of GO aqueous dispersion (1mg/mL) was mixed with 1mL of ammonia (28wt%) and 6mL of H2O2 (30wt%) under stirring. After 5min, the mixture was diluted to 160mL with deionized water and transferred to a 400mL Teflon autoclave and kept at 180°C for 8h. After that, the mixture was filtered through a nylon membrane (220nm) and the light-yellow supernatant was heated to remove the unreacted ammonia and H2O2. Finally, the product was freeze-dried and the yellow powders were collected to use later.

Amine-modified silica spheres were prepared by dispersing silica spheres (3g) into toluene solution (75mL). Subsequently, 2mL APTES was added to the mixture and stirred at 100°C for 24h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed with toluene, ethanol and methanol by filtration for several times and dried under vacuum at 60°C for 12h.

GQDs bonded silica composite was obtained by covalently coupling the carboxyl groups of GQDs and the amino groups of aminosilica with EDC/NHS as coupling agent. Specific process was as follows: First, 0.19g EDC (1mmol) and 0.11g NHS (1mmol) were added into 200mL of GQDs aqueous dispersion (1mg/mL) and the mixture was stirred for 30min to activate the carboxyl groups of GQDs. Then, 2.5g of aminosilica was added to the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 24h. Finally, the products were washed with water and methanol by filtration for several times and then were dried under vacuum at 60°C for 12h. The schematic diagram of the synthetic process was shown in Fig. 1 .

The GQDs bonded silica spheres (about 2.0g) were packed into stainless steel tubes (150 mm×4.6mm i.d.) by a slurry packing method under a constant packing pressure of 40MPa using 1,4-dioxane and carbon tetrachloride (1/1, v/v) as homogenate solvents and n-hexane as propulsion solvent.

All chromatographic separation was performed on a Waters HPLC system (Waters, USA), consisted of a Waters 515 HPLC pump, a Rheodyne 7725i injector equipped with 20μL sample loop and a Waters 2487 double λ absorbance UV detector. The chromatographic separation performance of GQDs/SiO2 column was evaluated in comparison with NH2-column, silica column and commercial C18 column (Phenomenex Luna 5μm C18(2) 100Å, 150 mm×4.6mm i.d.).

The morphologies of the prepared GQDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). The size distribution was figured out by measuring the size of one hundred of particles in the TEM images. Particle size statistics showed the diameters of GQDs were mainly distributed in the range of 16–27nm with a mean size of 21nm.

The chemical composition of GQDs was analyzed by FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectra (Fig. S1) indicated the presence of hydroxyl (3241cm−1), carbonyl (1719cm−1) and epoxy/ether (1093cm−1) at the prepared GQDs. In addition, the peak at 1423cm−1 was derived from CO symmetric stretching vibration of COO−. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. S2a) showed the predominant elements of the prepared GQDs were C, O and N. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. S2b) could be divided into four components, corresponding to the signals of CC (284.8eV), CO (286.5eV), CO (288.5eV) and OCO (289.2eV). The high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. S2c) could be divided into three components, corresponding to quinine (531.3eV), CO (532.1eV), and CO (533.2eV). These data were consistent with the data previously reported [24]. Based on the above results, we roughly concluded the main oxygen-containing functional groups of the prepared GQDs were OH, COOH and epoxy/ether.

Anilines, phenols, PAHs and aromatic ethers were chosen as test probes to investigate the chromatographic performance and retention mechanism of GQDs/SiO2 column in RP mode. C18 column was used as a comparison. The chromatograms for the separation of anilines and phenols using GQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column were presented in Fig. 5 . The tested anilines were almost baseline separated on GQDs/SiO2 column under the conditions of mobile phase containing 10% methanol within 10min. The α and Rs data were listed in Table 1 . However, p-methylaniline and o-nitroaniline were not separated on C18 column within the same analysis time.

The retention orders of these anilines were different on the two columns. This phenomenon roots in the different retention mechanisms between GQDs/SiO2 column and C18 column. The separation ability of C18 column depends on its hydrophobic property. However, besides hydrophobic interaction, GQDs can form π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and π-π electron-donor-acceptor (EDA) interaction [11,25] with analytes because they possess highly delocalized conjugate system of π-electron and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. For example, m-nitroaniline is supposed to show a weaker retention than p-methylaniline because (1) m-nitroaniline forms weaker hydrophobicity interaction with GQDs and (2) it forms weaker π-π interaction with GQDs since nitro group is a strong electron withdrawing group which will decrease the π-electron density of aromatic ring. However, the retention of m-nitroaniline was stronger than p-methylaniline on GQDs/SiO2 column. This result can be explained by π-π EDA interaction. On this occasion, GQDs acted as electron-donor because it had rich π-electron and m-nitroaniline acted as electron-acceptor because nitro group had strong electron withdrawing effect. This result was in accord with the previous research [25,26] that the π-π EDA interaction was proposed to explain the strong adsorption of nitroaromatics by CNTs and G. Moreover, the π-π EDA interaction facilitated the separation of certain compounds such as p-methylaniline and o-nitroaniline which were not separated on C18 column.

Fig. 5B showed the chromatograms of five phenols separated on GQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. These five phenolic compounds were better separated on GQDs/SiO2 column with much less dosage of methanol in mobile phase and shorter analysis time in comparison with C18 column. Similarly, different retention mechanisms led to the different retention orders. P-tert-butylphenol, which had strong hydrophobicity, had the longest retention time on C18 column. However, due to the π-π EDA interaction, p-nitrophenol had longer retention time than p-tert-butylphenol on GQDs/SiO2 column. The π-π stacking interaction was also reflected on GQDs/SiO2 column. 2-naphenol had stronger retention than p-tert-butylphenol because it formed stronger π-π stacking interaction with GQDs. With the help of π-π EDA and π-π stacking interaction, analytes with similar hydrophobicity achieved better separation. According to the experimental results, it was found that GQDs formed hydrophobic, π-π EDA and π-π stacking interaction with analytes and the multiple retention interactions played an important role during the separation process.

Fig. 6 presented the chromatograms for the separation of PAHs and aromatic ethers using GQDs/SiO2 column and C18 column. The four PAHs were not efficiently separated on C18 column but achieved good separation on GQDs/SiO2 column with the help of π-π stacking interaction. Moreover, shorter analysis time and less organic reagent were used for the separation of these PAHs using GQDs/SiO2 column. The four PAHs were also separated on GO bonded column with high proportion of acetonitrile as mobile phase and gradient elution device [11]. Separation results from GQDs and GO bonded columns showed that GQDs bonded column presented better separation performance for PAHs and used less dosage of organic reagent under isocratic elution. Through such comparison we may find that the π-π stacking property of GQDs plays a more effective role during the separation process of PAHs.

From Fig. 6B we can obviously see the inverse retention orders of aromatic ethers on the two columns. First, anisole had stronger retention than benzene on GQDs/SiO2 column because it formed stronger π-π stacking interaction with GQDs. Methoxy group was an electron-donating group which increased the π-electron density of aromatic ring, thus enhanced the π-π stacking interaction between anisole and GQDs. Second, o-nitroanisole had the strongest retention on GQDs/SiO2 column because it formed π-π EDA interaction with GQDs. This experiment result further demonstrated the multiple retention interactions of GQDs/SiO2 column.

Phenols, anilines and PAHs were also used as probes to evaluate the chromatographic performance of GQDs/SiO2 column in NP mode. The chromatograms for the separation of five phenols using GQDs/SiO2 column and silica column were presented in Fig. S3. It was found that GQDs/SiO2 column showed stronger retention property and more serious tailing phenomenon than silica column. This may due to the extra strong hydrogen-bond interaction between phenols and stationary phase since there are abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl on the surface of GQDs. As supporting evidence, four PAHs which could not form hydrogen bond interaction with stationary phase showed almost no peak tailing when separated on GQDs/SiO2 column.

Fig. 7 showed the chromatograms for the separation of anilines and PAHs on GQDs/SiO2 column and silica column. Through the comparison we saw that aniline and 3,4-dimethylaniline acquired better separation on GQDs/SiO2 column. This was due to the π-π stacking interaction supplied by GQDs. 3,4-dimethylaniline, with the methyl electron-donating group, had larger π-electron density and thus formed stronger π-π stacking interaction with GQDs than aniline. Four PAHs were completely separated on GQDs/SiO2 column. However, they couldn’t be separated on silica column because of similar and weak retention. The experiment results indicated that the π-π stacking property existed in GQDs/SiO2 column facilitated the separation of analytes with similar polarity in NP mode.

Alkaloids, nucleosides and nucleobases were chosen as probes to evaluate the separation performance of GQDs/SiO2 column in HILIC mode. As shown in Fig. 8 , six alkaloids, five nucleosides and four nucleobases were completely separated on GQDs/SiO2 column. To embody the separation effect of GQDs, NH2-SiO2 column was used as control under the identical chromatographic conditions. From the comparison images (Fig. 8a, b) we found NH2-SiO2 column and GQDs/SiO2 column showed different retention and separation performance toward to the six alkaloids. The retention orders of these six alkaloids were almost opposite on the two columns, which may be due to the opposite electrification state of stationary phases. Under the condition of pH 6.8, the alkaloids were almost positively charged, the surface of GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase were negatively charged with COO−, while the NH2-SiO2 stationary phase were positively charged with −NH3 +, so the stronger electrostatic attraction on GQDs/SiO2 column, the weaker retention on NH2-SiO2 column. In addition, the GQDs/SiO2 column presented better separation capacity for corydine and isocorydine which could not be separated on NH2-SiO2 column under the identical chromatographic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of water content on retention
ce:section.ce:para: Water content in the mobile phase has the largest effect on retention of polar compounds in HILIC since the hydrophilic interaction is enhanced by decreasing the polarity of eluent [27]. Four nucleosides were selected as probes to investigate the hydrophilic performance of the GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase. A range of 5–30% water content in mobile phase was studied while keeping ammonium acetate concentration constant at 10mM with pH 6.8. As shown in Fig. 9 , the logarithmic capacity factors (k) of the four nucleosides decreased with the increase of water content in mobile phase, which was consistent with the typical characteristic of HILIC.

The retention mechanism of HILIC is a complex process involving partitioning and surface adsorption. The relationship that is established for partitioning and surface adsorption can be described in Eqs. (1) and (2) respectively.
(1) log k =log kw − S φ

where kw is the capacity factor for the weaker eluent component only as mobile phase, φ is the volume fraction of the stronger member of a binary mobile phase mixture, and S is the slope of log k versus φ when fitted to a linear regression model [28].
(2)
log k = log
k B
−
A s
n B
log
N B
where kB is the solute retention factor with pure B as eluent, AS and nB are the cross-sectional areas occupied by the solute molecule on the surface and the B molecules, respectively, and NB is the mole fraction of the stronger member B in the eluent [28].

Fig. 9A displayed the dependence of log k on the volume fraction of water, which fitted well to the partitioning Eq. (1) at a range of 10–30% water content with correlation coefficients of 0.990–0.997. When the water content was below 10%, the log k increased dramatically because of the extraordinarily strong hydrophilic interaction, which was consistent with the phenomenon reported by other articles [27,29]. The correlation of log k and the logarithmic mole fraction of water showed correlation coefficients of 0.989–0.999 to the adsorption Eq. (2) at a range of 5–30% water content. According to the experiment results, both partitioning and adsorption retention mechanism played important role on GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of buffer concentration on retention
ce:section.ce:para: Since the retention mechanism of HILIC is a complex process of partitioning and electrostatic adsorption, buffer concentration affects retention in two ways: First, the stationary phase is occupied by a water-rich layer when using acetonitrile-water containing 95–70% (v/v) acetonitrile as mobile phase, high percentage of acetonitrile makes salt tend to partition into the water-rich layer, the increase of salt concentration in the mobile phase makes the increase of ions in the water-rich layer and thus the enhanced hydrophilic interaction with polar compounds; Second, the increase of salt concentration in the mobile phase restrains the electrostatic interaction between stationary phase and ionic compounds. The end result is decided by the two aspects together. In this study, six alkaloids were chosen as test probes to investigate the effect of buffer concentration on retention at pH 6.8 with ammonium acetate concentration at 5mM, 10mM, 15mM, 20mM, 25mM and 30mM (Fig. S4). The retention behavior didn’t show obvious regular changes with the salt concentration increased from 5mM to 15mM because of the combined effect of partitioning and electrostatic interactions. But when salt concentration was greater than 15mM, the retention appeared a decreasing trend, which was probably due to the diminished electrostatic attraction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of buffer pH on retention
ce:section.ce:para: Buffer pH also plays an important role on retention by influencing solute and stationary phase ionization. Six alkaloids were chosen as test probes to investigate the effect of buffer pH on retention by adjusting the pH of ammonium acetate aqueous solutions to 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 6.0 and 6.8 while keeping the concentration of ammonium acetate constant at 10mM (Fig. S5). With the rise of pH, the amount of COO− on the surface of GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase will increase, while the protonated NH+ of solute may reduce, which lead to the increased or reduced electrostatic interaction. The pKa value of caffeine, theobromine, isocorydine, corydine, protopine and berberine is 14, 13.3, 11.75, 9.68, 8.28 and 10 respectively [30,31]. Their ionization states change little over the pH range studied, however, the negative charges of stationary phase increase with the rise of pH, so the electrostatic attraction should be enhanced between solute and stationary phase, thus the increased retention time should be observed with the rise of pH. As we surmised, the retention time of protopine and berberine increased distinctly with the rise of pH. For caffeine and theobromine, their retention also slightly increased. But for corydine and isocorydine, their retention presented a downward trend. This may be due to the restricted hydrogen bonding interaction between phenolic hydroxyl group of corydine/isocorydine and stationary phase with the rise of pH, since hydrogen bonding interaction played a very important role for the retention of phenols just as described in the previous chapter.

The column efficiencies for all the test compounds were calculated. We found anilines and phenols achieved relatively higher column efficiency in the RP mode and the maximum column efficiency reached 29444 plates/m for 2-naphenol. However, higher column efficiency was obtained for PAHs in NP mode with maximum value of 46034 plates/m for fluorene whose column efficiency was only 7707 plates/m in RP mode. In contrast to RP and NP, under HILIC conditions high column efficiencies were achieved for all polar compounds.

The run-to-run and day-to-day repeatability and column-to-column reproducibility of GQDs/SiO2 column were tested by the separation of PAHs, anilines and nucleobases in NP, RP and HILIC mode respectively. The repeatability and reproducibility of the column in the three separation modes was satisfactory. The RSD values of the k were less than 0.66% (n=8, run-to-run), 1.82% (n=3, day-to-day) and 5.24% (n=3, column-to-column) (Table S2). The run-to-run overlapped chromatograms were shown in Fig. S6.

GQDs bonded silica stationary phase was prepared by coupling the carboxyl groups of GQDs and the amino groups of aminosilica. Several kinds of aromatic compounds including anilines, phenols and PAHs were efficiently separated on this GQDs/SiO2 column in NP and RP mode. Through comparing its retention behavior with C18 column and silica column, it was found that GQDs could form hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking, π-π EDA and hydrogen bond interaction with analytes. Moreover, low percentage of methanol was used as mobile phase in RP mode, which showed great superiority in economy and environmental protection. Six alkaloids, five nucleosides and four nucleobases were completely separated on this GQDs/SiO2 column in HILIC mode. Through investigating the effect of water content, buffer concentration and pH on retention time, it was found that this new stationary phase showed a complex retention mechanism of partitioning, adsorption and electrostatic interaction in HILIC mode. Our research indicates that GQDs are efficient multifunctional separation material and have broad prospect in separation science.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (Nos. 21405162, 21405161 and 20675085), the Key Projects of Urumqi Science and Technology Bureau (Y141320007) and the top priority program of “One-Three-Five” Strategic Planning of Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, CAS.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.chroma.2017.02.067.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanotube composite microspheres as a highly efficient solid-phase microextraction coating for sensitive determination of phthalate acid esters in water samples

Determination of six sulfonylurea herbicides in environmental water samples by magnetic solid-phase extraction using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as adsorbents coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography

Preparation of a novel ionic hybrid stationary phase by non-covalent functionalization of single-walled carbon nanotubes with amino-derivatized silica gel for fast HPLC separation of aromatic compounds
Development of a C-60-fullerene bonded open-tubular capillary using a photo/thermal active agent for liquid chromatographic separations by pi–pi interactions
Self-assembled covalent capillary coating of diazoresin/carboxyl fullerene for analysis of proteins by capillary electrophoresis and a comparison with diazoresin/graphene oxide coating
A novel fullerene oxide functionalized silica composite as stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography
Nanodiamond-Decorated silica spheres as a chromatographic material
Pellicular particles with spherical carbon cores and porous nanodiamond/polymer shells for reversed-phase HPLC
A new nano-on-micro stationary phase based on nanodiamond bonded on silica for hydrophilic interaction chromatography
Layer-by-layer self-assembled graphene oxide/silica microsphere composites as stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography
Preparation and retention mechanism study of graphene and graphene oxide bonded silica microspheres as stationary phases for high performance liquid chromatography
Magnetized graphene layers synthesized on the carbon nanofibers as novel adsorbent for the extraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from environmental water samples
Progress in stationary phases modified with carbonaceous nanomaterials for high-performance liquid chromatography
Hydrothermal preparation of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots characterized excitation-Independent emission and its application as a bioimaging reagent
Electrochemical synthesis of small-sized red fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a bioimaging platform
Photoluminescent sensing for acidic amino acids based on the disruption of graphene quantum dots/europium ions aggregates
Graphene quantum dots as additives in capillary electrophoresis for separation cinnamic acid and its derivatives
Facile synthesis of analogous graphene quantum dots with sp2 hybridized carbon atom dominant structures and their photovoltaic application

Preparation and characterization of Fe3O4/graphene quantum dots nanocomposite as an efficient adsorbent in magnetic solid phase extraction: application to determination of bisphenol A in water samples
Ultrafast adsorption and selective desorption of aqueous aromatic dyes by graphene sheets modified by graphene quantum dots
Capillary column coated with graphene quantum dots for gas chromatographic separation of alkanes and aromatic isomers
Layer-by-layer assembly of ultrathin composite films from micron-sized graphite oxide sheets and polycations
Flexible graphene films via the filtration of water-soluble noncovalent functionalized graphene sheets
Insight into the formation mechanism of graphene quantum dots and the size effect on their electrochemical behaviors
Characterization of aromatic compound sorptive interactions with black carbon (Charcoal) assisted by graphite as a model
Layer-by-layer fabrication of chemical-bonded graphene coating for solid-phase microextraction
Imidazoline type stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography and reversed-phase liquid chromatography
Carbon nanoparticles from corn stalk soot and its novel application as stationary phase of hydrophilic interaction chromatography and per aqueous liquid chromatography
Ultraviolet-visible study on acid-base equilibria of aporphine alkaloids with antiplasmodial and antioxidant activities from Alseodaphne corneri and Dehaasia longipedicellata
Retention behaviour of selected alkaloids on bonded stationary phases by HPLC
